description:
Let patients choose whether to enable MFA, with supported factors limited to email OTP and SMS OTP. Do not require authenticator-app/TOTP for launch.

Context
- We temporarily disabled required Cognito MFA because new patients were forced into authenticator-app setup.
- Desired posture is optional, patient-managed MFA: patients can turn MFA on or off, choose email or SMS when eligible, and sign in through the selected challenge.
- AWS Cognito optional MFA is app-owned: managed login does not prompt setup when MFA is optional, so Apoth must provide the setup/preference UI and challenge handling.
- Email MFA has Cognito constraints around SES-backed email configuration, Essentials/Plus feature plan availability, and password-reset recovery method conflicts. SMS MFA also requires phone-number collection/verification and SNS/SMS sandbox or spend-limit readiness.

Scope
- Change Cognito user-pool infrastructure from MFA off to optional only when email/SMS factors can be supported without enabling TOTP as a patient-facing option.
- Configure or document any required Cognito account-recovery changes so email MFA does not strand users who rely on email-only password reset.
- Add patient account/security UI for MFA status, enable, disable, and preferred factor selection.
- Support email OTP and SMS OTP sign-in challenges in the browser auth facade without exposing raw Cognito sessions, challenge IDs, tokens, provider errors, phone/email values beyond normal form fields, or provider internals.
- Add phone-number collection/update and verification only as needed for SMS MFA; keep it out of Stripe metadata and clinical/MDI payloads.
- Do not store MFA codes, workflow URLs/tokens, questionnaire answers, clinical content, raw Cognito challenge sessions, or PHI-heavy payloads in Apoth state/logs.
- Keep authenticator-app/TOTP disabled or unsupported in patient UI for launch.
- Include rollback/operations notes for what happens to existing users if MFA settings change after deployment.

Acceptance
- New users are not forced into MFA during sign-up or first sign-in.
- A signed-in patient can enable MFA with email OTP if the Cognito pool/account supports it and password recovery remains viable.
- A signed-in patient can enable MFA with SMS OTP after providing/verifying a phone number, subject to SMS sandbox/production readiness.
- A signed-in patient can disable MFA and can switch preferred MFA method between supported factors when both are available.
- Sign-in handles EMAIL_OTP and SMS_MFA challenges with patient-safe copy and no raw Cognito/session/token leakage.
- Password reset still has a valid recovery path for users whose preferred MFA is email or SMS.
- Focused unit/component/infra tests cover optional MFA config, preference changes, email/SMS challenge handling, and non-disclosure of provider internals.
title: Optional Cognito email/SMS MFA self-service
